title: Schankian Primitives ARE Image Schemas
subtitle: The surprising 1:1 mapping between Conceptual Dependency and Lakoff's embodied cognition

children: Two researchers, decades apart, independently discovered the same set of cognitive primitives — one from story understanding (Schank, 1972), the other from embodied metaphor (Lakoff, 1987). This tutorial explores why they converged and what it means for computational semantics.

props.title: Two Roads to the Same Primitives

children: Roger Schank asked: "What are the irreducible operations that all stories are made of?" He arrived at 11 Conceptual Dependency (CD) primitives — things like PTRANS (physical transfer), PROPEL (apply force), INGEST (take something in).
George Lakoff asked: "What are the pre-conceptual structures that all abstract thought is built on?" He arrived at image schemas — PATH, FORCE_DYNAMICS, CONTAINER, TRANSFER, and about 25 others.

props.source: Two cartographers mapping the same continent from different coastlines
props.target: Two cognitive scientists discovering the same primitives from different starting points (narrative vs. embodiment)

children: The striking thing: these aren't vaguely similar. They map 1:1.

items.definition: Physical transfer of an object from one location to another. Both encode: source, route, goal.
Abstract transfer of possession/control. Both encode: donor, recipient, object transferred.
Application of physical force to an object. Both encode: agonist, antagonist, force vector, result.
Movement of a body part. PATH schema restricted to the agent's own body.
Taking something into the body. CONTAINER schema where the body is the container and something enters.
Putting something out of the body. CONTAINER schema where something exits.
Mental transfer of information between minds. Information moves from one mind-container to another.
Constructing a thought from components. Assembling parts into a coherent whole.
Focusing a sense organ. Bringing something from periphery to center of attention.
Producing sound/language. Creating a connection (LINK) via signal transfer.
Understanding/comprehending. A concept 'fits' an existing framework — structural correspondence.

children: The image schemas aren't just relabeled CDs — they're RICHER. Each schema carries internal structure that CDs leave implicit:

Schank's INGEST just says: actor takes object into body. But CONTAINER gives us: interior (bounded region), exterior (everything outside), boundary (the membrane/wall), entry (crossing boundary inward), exit (crossing boundary outward), capacity (how full). This structure explains WHY 'she's full of ideas' works as a metaphor — the CONTAINER schema was already there, ready to be projected onto MIND.

Schank's PROPEL just says: actor applies force to object. FORCE_DYNAMICS (from Talmy) gives us: agonist (entity exerting force), antagonist (entity resisting), force tendency (toward motion or rest), balance of forces, result (motion or stasis). This explains 'she pushed through the difficulty' — the difficulty is the ANTAGONIST, she is the AGONIST, and the result is MOTION despite resistance.

props.title: What does this mean for HRR encoding?

children: If we encode operators as HRR vectors, the image schema structure tells us WHAT to encode. Instead of a flat 'PROPEL' vector, we encode: bind(agonist_role, entity_A) + bind(antagonist_role, entity_B) + bind(force_direction, vector) + bind(result, motion_or_stasis). The schema structure becomes the BINDING PATTERN of the HRR vector. Different operators are distinguished not by arbitrary dimensions but by which roles they fill.

Here's where the isomorphism becomes practically powerful. Lakoff showed that abstract thought works by PROJECTING image schemas from concrete domains to abstract ones. If CDs ARE image schemas, then:

Every conceptual metaphor is a REUSE of a Schankian primitive in a new domain. 'ARGUMENT IS WAR' means: apply PROPEL/FORCE_DYNAMICS schema to the domain of argumentation. The same cognitive operation that handles physical force now handles persuasive force.

props.title: Metaphor as Schema Projection
props.description: See how one image schema generates multiple figurative expressions across domains

children: Our wake-sleep compression system (arXiv:2603.25975) rediscovered Schankian primitives from narrative data. If CDs ARE image schemas, this means:

items.definition: The system isn't finding the most efficient encoding of language — it's finding the most efficient encoding of EXPERIENCE. Image schemas come from sensorimotor interaction with the physical world.

Three independent paths (Schank from stories, Lakoff from metaphor, our compression from statistics) all arrive at the same structures. This suggests they're genuinely fundamental, not an artifact of any one methodology.
If operators ARE image schemas, they're already grounded in embodied experience. No separate grounding step needed — the primitive IS the grounding.

props.title: ISL^FOL: Formalizing image schemas as logic

children: Hedblom et al. developed Image Schema Logic (ISL^FOL), which encodes image schemas as first-order logic formulas. CONTAINER(x) ↔ ∃interior, boundary, exterior: inside(x, interior) ∧ bounded_by(interior, boundary). This maps directly to our lambda store format — each schema becomes a typed lambda term. The isomorphism isn't just conceptual; it's formally expressible.
If this isomorphism is real, we can make a concrete prediction:

Take Lakoff's 202 conceptual metaphors from the Master Metaphor List. For each, identify the source domain. Map the source domain to its primary image schema. Check: does that schema correspond to one of Schank's 11 CDs? Prediction: >80% will map cleanly to 1-2 CDs.

Preliminary analysis of the first 50 metaphors from the Event Structure section shows near-perfect mapping: every metaphor involving motion maps to PTRANS/PATH, every metaphor involving force maps to PROPEL/FORCE_DYNAMICS, every metaphor involving containment maps to INGEST-EXPEL/CONTAINER.

This isomorphism has an immediate practical application: storing figurative language in a knowledge system. Instead of memorizing every idiom individually, we store GENERATIVE MAPPINGS — the conceptual metaphor rules that produce unlimited figurative expressions.

1. Parse: TORPEDO(agent, deal) — but deals aren't physical objects
2. Detect: literal interpretation fails (type mismatch)
3. Look up: which generative mapping contains 'torpedo'?
4. Find: ARGUMENT_IS_WAR → {torpedo: 'destroy_suddenly'}
5. Interpret: DESTROY_SUDDENLY(agent, deal)
6. Ground: via FORCE_DYNAMICS schema (sudden overwhelming force)

One stored mapping rule → unlimited figurative comprehension.

props.title: Fiber Selection: Literal vs. Figurative
props.description: The same word activates different fibers depending on context

questions.question: Which image schema does Schank's INGEST primitive correspond to?
questions.explanation: INGEST = taking something INTO the body. The body is the CONTAINER, and the ingested substance crosses the boundary inward. This is why 'absorbing information' works as a metaphor — the CONTAINER schema projects from body to mind.

questions.question: Why are image schemas considered RICHER than Schank's CDs?
options: They carry internal topological structure (boundaries, vectors, capacities)
questions.explanation: Image schemas encode internal structure: CONTAINER has interior/exterior/boundary/capacity, PATH has source/route/goal/direction, FORCE_DYNAMICS has agonist/antagonist/tendency/result. CDs name the operation but leave this structure implicit.

questions.question: What does the CD↔Lakoff isomorphism imply about wake-sleep compression?
options: Compression rediscovers pre-conceptual structures of embodied cognition
Compression requires image schema labels as training signal
Only English narratives produce Schankian primitives
questions.explanation: If three independent approaches (Schank from stories, Lakoff from metaphor, wake-sleep from statistics) all converge on the same structures, these primitives reflect something fundamental about how minds encode experience — not just an artifact of any one method.

questions.question: How does a generative mapping handle 'she torpedoed the deal'?
options: Memorizes 'torpedo the deal' as a fixed idiom
Projects FORCE_DYNAMICS schema via ARGUMENT_IS_WAR mapping: torpedo → destroy_suddenly
Translates word-by-word from military to business vocabulary
questions.explanation: The system looks up 'torpedo' in stored generative mappings, finds it in ARGUMENT_IS_WAR with meaning 'destroy_suddenly', and interprets the whole expression. One mapping rule → unlimited figurative expressions using war vocabulary.
